title: Chapter 1: Suffocation
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D7+D10 -->
Her throat was closing.

Not metaphorically — not the way people said it when they meant stressed or overwhelmed or kind of freaked out. Her actual throat was narrowing, cartilage squeezing, like a hand had reached through her collar and was slowly, politely, strangling her from the inside. Wren's fingers dug into the desk. The tablet stylus rolled off and clattered somewhere she couldn't see because the screen was blurring, pixels bleeding into light, the whole apartment warping at the edges like a corrupted file.
She couldn't breathe.

She couldn't breathe and the notification light was strobing — three pulses, pause, three pulses — and each pulse was a siren she couldn't mute because the sound wasn't coming from the speakers, it was coming from inside her chest, that rabbit-heart slam against ribs that meant something terrible was happening or about to happen or already had happened and she hadn't stopped it yet.
*Inhale.*

She tried. Her lungs seized. The overhead light buzzed, a frequency that lived in her molars, and the rain hit the window in alternating sheets like the city was breathing too — wet and uneven and suffocating right along with her.
*Just breathe. Just—*
The screen cracked back into focus. Three new messages from Marcus Holt.
She read the first one.
**Re: FINAL final revisions — URGENT**

*"Wren. This composition is, and I'm being generous, amateurish. The color story reads like a child's crayon drawing. I need you to redo the entire third panel sequence and I need it by 6 AM. I don't care what time it is. You committed to this project. Act like it."*

2:47 AM. He'd sent it at 2:47 AM and expected it by 6 AM and she'd already done three revision rounds this week, each one longer and more demoralizing than the last, and the third panel sequence had taken her eleven hours the first time.
Her hands were shaking.
She opened the second message.
**Re: Re: FINAL final revisions — URGENT**
*"Also the line weight on the secondary figure is inconsistent. I shouldn't have to point this out. Do better."*
Third message. Same thread. No greeting.
**Re: Re: Re: FINAL final revisions — URGENT**
*"I'm waiting."*

Sent at 3:02 AM. Fifteen minutes ago. The read receipt had already delivered itself like a knife to the gut — *seen 3:03 AM* — and she hadn't replied because replying meant words and words meant the possibility of saying the wrong thing and saying the wrong thing meant he'd email again, harder, and she couldn't, she couldn't, she—
Stop.

She pressed her palms flat against the desk. The laminate was cold. She held them there until the cold bit through the tablet calluses on her right hand, until sensation grounded her enough to realize she'd stopped breathing less and was now breathing more, which was the opposite of dying, probably.

The apartment was small. A studio carved into something that pretended to be a one-bedroom by virtue of a half-wall that didn't reach the ceiling and a curtain rod that held no curtain. Neon bled through the rain-streaked window — magenta from the ramen shop below, electric blue from the clinic across the street, intermittent white from delivery drones scanning the building for drop slots. The light shifted like a slow glitch, painting the walls in colors that didn't belong to anyone.

Her noise-canceling headphones sat around her neck, drained of charge, useless. She'd forgotten to plug them in. Again. The city's night-frequency filtered through the glass — drone hum, rain percussion, someone's bass-heavy music from two floors up, the algorithmic chime of a notification from a device she couldn't identify.
Everything was loud. Everything was watching.
She opened her algo-feed.

She didn't mean to. Her thumb moved on muscle memory and there it was, the infinite scroll, every post a mirror she didn't want to look into. A classmate's book deal announcement. A mutual's engagement photo. Someone she'd met once at a gallery opening, now with 400K followers and a brand partnership, smiling with teeth that gleamed like they'd been rendered in post.
*Congrats!!* someone had commented. *So happy for you!!* *Living the dream!*

Wren's thumb kept scrolling. Post after post after post, each one a small bright window into a life where people existed visibly, unapologetically, without the constant hum of dread that turned every potential action into a cost-benefit analysis of how badly they'd regret it later.

Her own feed had been silent for six days. Her last post — a sketch she'd spent two hours overthinking and three more debating whether to delete — had gotten fourteen likes and one comment from a bot.
She scrolled faster. The images smeared together. Bright, bright, bright. Everyone so bright.
Her phone buzzed. Different app. Different notification.
**Deadline Reminder: Helios Children's Foundation — Illustration submission due 11:59 PM**
She stared at it. The date said June 11th. Today was June 12th.
She'd missed it.

The Helios commission — the one she'd actually wanted, the one that meant something, the children's hospital mural design that could've been a real line on a real portfolio instead of Marcus Holt's disposable commercial garbage — had been due last night at midnight and she'd been so deep in Marcus's revision tunnel that she'd let it lapse.
The stylus was still on the floor. She didn't pick it up.
Mayfair's door opened.

No — not opened. Slid. Mayfair had installed a smart-slide mechanism on the bedroom door last month, some influencer discount code thing, so now the door announced itself with a soft pneumatic hiss that sounded like the apartment was sighing in resignation.
Mayfair Chen emerged in a silk robe the color of arterial blood, Ring Light Prime clutched in one hand, phone rig in the other, already talking before she'd fully entered the room.

"—and honestly, you guys, the night aesthetic in this apartment is *unreal*, like, look at this —" She pivoted, sweeping the camera across the living space, and Wren existed in the frame for exactly one second before Mayfair adjusted the angle, a micro-correction that excluded the desk, the shaking girl, the dead headphones, all of it.
Wren pulled her hoodie tighter. The fabric smelled like dryer sheets and anxiety sweat.

"So my skincare routine is officially entering its midnight era," Mayfair continued to her eight hundred thousand followers, settling onto the couch with the practiced ease of someone who'd been performing for cameras since before she could drive. "The hydrating serum is giving, the overnight mask is giving, obviously the LED panel is giving — oh, and before you ask, yes, the new lip tint, shade Malice, link in bio."
She held up her face to the ring light. Her skin glowed. Her pores did not exist.

Wren's gaze swept back to the window. Outside, a drone banked past the glass, its sensor eye scanning the building's facade — red dot, green dot, red — and for a moment the red dot hovered directly at her face before sliding away. Standard delivery run. Probably. The city was full of cameras that were full of algorithms that were full of judgments she'd never see.

She got up from the desk. Moved to the corner of the apartment where the half-wall created a kind of alcove — not a room, not a hiding spot, more of an architectural apology. Her drawing pad was wedged behind the storage ottoman. She pulled it out, flipped to a fresh page, and started sketching without thinking.
A figure. Hooded. Small. Surrounded by rectangles — screens, phones, windows, all of them flickering with the same strobing light.

Her hand moved fast. Charcoal-on-paper fast, even though she was using a frayed eraser-pencil she'd found at the bottom of a drawer three months ago. The lines came out aggressive. The figure's shoulders hiked up toward where ears would be. The rectangles multiplied, tessellating like grid tiles, like the algo-feed, like every bright loud thing that demanded she look at it, respond to it, exist inside it.
She drew until her hand cramped. Then she looked at what she'd made.

It was her. Obviously. The figure was her and the screens were the world and the posture was the same one she'd been holding for months, years, this crouch-shape that her spine had apparently decided was permanent now.
Even the drawing felt exposed.
She tore the page out, folded it, shoved it under the ottoman.
Mayfair was still filming. The ring light cast a perfect white circle on the ceiling, a surveillance moon that made the rest of the apartment feel even dimmer by contrast.
"—and that's my midnight routine, besties. Remember: glow is a lifestyle, not a product. Love you, mean it, kiss kiss."

The recording light blinked off. Mayfair examined her thumbnail, grimaced, deleted something, started typing. She didn't look at Wren. She never looked at Wren unless she needed something — rent, a package accepted, a body to fill the apartment so it didn't look empty on her story.
Wren's phone buzzed again. She flinched. Checked it.
Marcus.
**Re: Re: Re: Re: FINAL final revisions — URGENT**

*"Your silence is noted. I've cc'd my producer on this thread. The full sequence needs to be in my inbox by 7 AM or we'll be having a very different conversation about your contract. I expect professionalism, Wren. For once."*
The cc line read: *V. Narayan, Senior Producer, Holt Creative Group.*

He'd escalated. He'd pulled in a producer at four in the morning to pressure a freelance illustrator into delivering three revision rounds in the space of a single night. And she couldn't say no because the contract — the contract she'd signed without reading carefully enough because reading it carefully would've required asking a question and asking a question would've required being the kind of person who could do that — the contract gave him the right to unlimited revisions within scope and his definition of scope was "whatever I say it is."
She typed back: *Will have it ready.*
Deleted it.
Typed: *I'll send the panels by 7.*
Deleted it.
Typed: *Yes, Marcus.*
Sent.

The taste in her mouth was metal and cowardice. She set the phone face-down on the desk and watched the screen flash through the back of the case, notifications still arriving, the algo-feed still refreshing, the city still buzzing and droning and glowing through the glass like a tumor that had learned to be beautiful.
Rain streaked the window. A drone passed. The neon below shifted from magenta to a sick violet as the ramen shop cycled its signage, and for a moment the whole apartment was the color of a bruise.
She opened her phone again. The feed had refreshed. Amid the noise — the posts, the stories, the reels she'd never watch — a sponsored card had inserted itself into the scroll.
White text on a soft gray background. Clean design. Medical, but gentle.
**"Social anxiety? New treatment available."**
Below it, smaller: *Neural modulation clinical trial. Accepting applicants 18–30. Confidential. Compensated.*
Wren's thumb hovered.

She read it again. *Confidential.* The word sat there like a door left slightly ajar, light leaking through from a room she'd never been inside. Confidential meant no one had to know. Confidential meant she could try something without it becoming content, without Mayfair filming her "healing journey," without Marcus using it as ammunition, without the algo-feed weighing in with its thousand bright opinions.
She screenshotted it.
The image saved to her folder. She closed the app. Stared at the ceiling, where Mayfair's ring light had left a retinal ghost, a white circle that pulsed even after she looked away.
The apartment hummed. The city churned. Somewhere in the walls, pipes groaned like the building was trying to swallow itself.
Wren didn't open the link. Not yet. She tucked herself into the alcove, pulled the ottoman closer, and sat in the half-dark with her phone balanced on her knee.
The screenshot was still there. A single image in a folder of ten thousand.
She went to bed.

Not sleep — she'd given up on that months ago. Just bed. The mattress was a loaner from Mayfair's previous apartment, soft in the wrong places, and the sheets were slightly damp because the window seals were ancient and the rain found its way in through cracks that the landlord had promised to fix in a lease that was also a promise, also broken.

She lay on her back. The ceiling was a map of water stains and old smoke detector holes, and the neon from outside painted it in shifting colors that moved like something alive, something watching, something that knew her name and was waiting for her to look back.
Her phone was still in her hand. The screen was dark but she could feel the weight of the screenshot inside it, that little thumbnail of possibility, compressed into pixels, waiting.
*Social anxiety?*
The question was so blunt it was almost funny. Like a diagnosis dispensed by a billboard. Like the city itself had lean-forward-whispered: *Hey. I see you. Want to stop feeling like this?*
Her thumb found the screen. Tapped. The photos app opened. The screenshot filled the display.
She studied the link at the bottom of the ad. A URL. Clean, no tracking parameters, no algo-tagging — just a simple address that led somewhere she'd never been.
Wren exhaled. The breath came out shaky, the kind of breath that could go either way — into another panic spiral or into something else, something that started with the smallest possible motion.
Her thumb pressed the link.
The page began to load.
